search_results.title: Google Crawling and Indexing | Documentation
search_results.snippet: Crawling and indexing help your site rank in search results. Browse topics to understand what it means for Google to crawl your website and index it.

search_results.title: Mobile-first Indexing Best Practices | Google Search Central
search_results.snippet: Google uses the mobile version of a site's content, crawled with the smartphone agent, for indexing and ranking. This is called mobile-first indexing.

search_results.title: In-Depth Guide to How Google Search Works | Documentation
search_results.snippet: Get an in-depth understanding of how Google Search works and improve your site for Google's crawling, indexing, and ranking processes.

search_results.title: Crawling, Indexing and Ranking | SEO for Beginners - YouTube
search_results.snippet: In the second part of our video series, “SEO For Beginners”, we talk about how search engines like Google crawl, index, and rank websites.

search_results.title: The Complete Guide to Google's Official SEO Documentation
search_results.snippet: Google breaks its process down into three primary stages: crawling, indexing, and serving search results. Crawling is the discovery phase.

message.content:
Below is a checklist-style enumeration of **testable crawlability & indexing checks** derived from **Google Search Central** (only official docs) with:

- The **check** (what to test in a web app)
- **What it verifies** (intent/impact)
- The **Google Search Central topic name**
- The **exact URL** (as requested)

I focus only on crawl/index-related requirements you listed: robots.txt, robots meta, X‑Robots‑Tag, sitemaps, crawl budget, URL structure, redirects, soft 404s, HTTP status codes, blocked resources.

---

### 1. robots.txt validity and directives

**Check 1.1 – robots.txt reachable and syntactically valid**

- **What it verifies**  
  That `https://example.com/robots.txt` is accessible (2xx) and uses valid syntax so Google can correctly interpret crawl rules.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“robots.txt”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots/intro`

**Check 1.2 – robots.txt does not block essential crawl paths**

- **What it verifies**  
  That important URL paths for HTML content are not disallowed for `User-agent: Googlebot` (and relevant vertical bots like `Googlebot-Image`, `Googlebot-Video`, etc.), preventing crawlers from discovering and fetching pages.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“robots.txt”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots/intro`

**Check 1.3 – robots.txt not used to block indexing of already-known URLs**

- **What it verifies**  
  That you are not relying on `robots.txt` to keep URLs out of the index (since robots.txt only controls crawling, not indexing of known URLs).[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“robots.txt”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots/intro`

**Check 1.4 – robots.txt rules consistent across mobile/desktop**

- **What it verifies**  
  That robots.txt rules (especially for critical paths) behave as intended for both mobile and desktop versions of the site, avoiding accidental blocking under mobile-first indexing.[2]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Mobile-first indexing best practices”** (robots.txt rules)  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/mobile/mobile-sites-mobile-first-indexing`

---

### 2. Robots meta tags (`noindex`, `nofollow`, `none`)

**Check 2.1 – Presence and correctness of robots meta tag values**

- **What it verifies**  
  That `<meta name="robots" content="…">` uses valid directives (`noindex`, `nofollow`, `none`, etc.) and is properly placed in the `<head>` so Google can parse it.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Robots meta tag, data-nosnippet, and X-Robots-Tag specifications”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-meta-tag`

**Check 2.2 – No unintended `noindex` on indexable pages**

- **What it verifies**  
  That important pages intended to appear in Search do **not** contain `noindex` (or equivalent `none`) robots meta tag, which would prevent indexing.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Block indexing with the noindex meta tag”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/block-indexing`

**Check 2.3 – Intended use of `nofollow`/`none` and link crawling**

- **What it verifies**  
  That when `nofollow` or `none` is used, it matches the intended behavior for not following links on that page, and doesn’t accidentally cut off discovery of deeper content you rely on for crawl.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Robots meta tag, data-nosnippet, and X-Robots-Tag specifications”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-meta-tag`

---

### 3. X‑Robots‑Tag HTTP header

**Check 3.1 – HTTP X‑Robots‑Tag used correctly on HTTP responses**

- **What it verifies**  
  That HTTP responses (HTML, PDFs, other media) use valid `X-Robots-Tag` directives (e.g., `noindex`, `nofollow`, `noarchive`) and that these directives are not contradicting robots meta tags or blocking important content.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Robots meta tag, data-nosnippet, and X-Robots-Tag specifications”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-meta-tag`

**Check 3.2 – Header directives consistent with indexing intent**

- **What it verifies**  
  That no critical HTML or resource URLs return `X-Robots-Tag: noindex` or similar by mistake (e.g., due to server config inherited across paths).[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Block indexing with the noindex meta tag”** (also describes header-based noindex)  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/block-indexing`

---

### 4. XML sitemap validity / format / size limits

**Check 4.1 – XML sitemap format validity**

- **What it verifies**  
  That sitemap files use a valid XML sitemap format (correct root element, namespace, `<urlset>`, `<url>`, `<loc>`, etc.) and are not malformed.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Sitemaps”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/sitemaps/overview`

**Check 4.2 – Sitemap URL discovery and accessibility**

- **What it verifies**  
  That sitemap URLs (e.g., `/sitemap.xml`) are accessible (2xx), not blocked by robots.txt, and can be retrieved by Google.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Sitemaps”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/sitemaps/overview`

**Check 4.3 – Sitemap size and URL-count limits**

- **What it verifies**  
  That each sitemap is within Google’s documented limits (e.g., maximum URLs per sitemap and maximum uncompressed size) and that larger sets use a sitemap index when needed.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Sitemaps”** (size and count constraints)  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/sitemaps/overview`

**Check 4.4 – Only canonical, indexable URLs in sitemaps**

- **What it verifies**  
  That sitemap URLs generally point to canonical, indexable pages (not non-canonical variants, 3xx, 4xx, `noindex`, or heavily parameterized duplicates), aligning with Google’s guidance on using sitemaps for important URLs.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Sitemaps”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/sitemaps/overview`

---

### 5. Sitemap submission

**Check 5.1 – Sitemap referenced via robots.txt**

- **What it verifies**  
  That the robots.txt file contains a `Sitemap:` directive pointing to the sitemap or sitemap index so Google can discover it automatically.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Sitemaps”** (discovery methods)  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/sitemaps/overview`

**Check 5.2 – Sitemap submitted in Search Console**

- **What it verifies**  
  That sitemaps are also submitted via Google Search Console’s Sitemaps feature to help Google find new or updated URLs.[1][6]  
- **Search Central topics**  
  - **“Sitemaps”**  
  - **“Search Console tools”**  
- **URLs**  
  - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/sitemaps/overview`  
  - `https://search.google.com/search-console/about`

---

### 6. Crawl budget (large sites / faceted navigation)

**Check 6.1 – Manage faceted navigation to avoid crawl traps**

- **What it verifies**  
  That faceted navigation and URL parameters do not generate infinite or near-infinite crawl spaces (e.g., combinatorial filters), and that such URLs are managed so they do not waste crawl budget.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Managing crawling of faceted navigation URLs”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/faceted-navigation`

**Check 6.2 – Large site crawl budget management**

- **What it verifies**  
  That large sites follow practices to avoid wasting crawl budget (e.g., reducing duplicate URLs, avoiding endless calendar pages, limiting low-value URLs) so important pages can be crawled more often.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Large site owner’s guide to managing your crawl budget”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/large-site-managing-crawl-budget`

**Check 6.3 – Server performance & response quality for crawling**

- **What it verifies**  
  That the server can handle Googlebot’s crawl rate without serving errors or very slow responses, which can reduce crawl rate and harm discovery.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Large site owner’s guide to managing your crawl budget”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/large-site-managing-crawl-budget`

**Check 6.4 – Use of proper canonicalization to reduce duplicate crawling**

- **What it verifies**  
  That canonical tags and other canonicalization techniques are used so Google can focus crawling on canonical URLs, rather than multiple duplicates.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Canonicalization”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/canonicalization`

---

### 7. URL structure

**Check 7.1 – Logical, human-readable URL structure**

- **What it verifies**  
  That URLs are organized in a logical hierarchy, descriptive, and human-intelligible, which helps both users and Google understand site structure.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“URL structure”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/url-structure`

**Check 7.2 – Consistent, stable URLs without unnecessary parameters**

- **What it verifies**  
  That URLs do not contain unnecessary session IDs or changing parameters that create many duplicates and complicate crawling and indexing.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“URL structure”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/url-structure`

**Check 7.3 – One primary URL per piece of content (canonicalization)**

- **What it verifies**  
  That each piece of content is primarily available on a single, preferred URL, with duplicates indicating the canonical via rel=canonical or other signals to help indexing.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Canonicalization”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/canonicalization`

---

### 8. Redirect chains

**Check 8.1 – Avoid long redirect chains and loops**

- **What it verifies**  
  That URLs used internally or in sitemaps are not sending users and crawlers through long 3xx chains or loops, which waste crawl budget and may prevent proper indexing.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Redirects and Google Search”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/redirects-and-google-search`

**Check 8.2 – Correct use of 301/302 for site moves or URL changes**

- **What it verifies**  
  That permanent moves are implemented as 301 redirects, temporary as 302 when appropriate, helping Google consolidate indexing and signals correctly.[1]  
- **Search Central topics**  
  - **“Redirects and Google Search”**  
  - **“Site moves”**  
- **URLs**  
  - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/redirects-and-google-search`  
  - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/site-move-with-url-changes`

---

### 9. Soft 404s

**Check 9.1 – Pages that look like 404s return proper 404/410**

- **What it verifies**  
  That pages which display “not found” or error content do not return 200 OK but instead return a proper 404 or 410 HTTP status, avoiding soft 404 classification and index bloat.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“How HTTP status codes, and network and DNS errors affect Google Search”** (soft 404s)  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/http-network-errors`

**Check 9.2 – Legitimate content not misclassified as soft 404**

- **What it verifies**  
  That thin or template-like pages still provide enough unique content and correct status codes to avoid being treated as soft 404s.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“How HTTP status codes, and network and DNS errors affect Google Search”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/http-network-errors`

---

### 10. HTTP status codes for crawl and index

**Check 10.1 – Indexable content returns 200 OK**

- **What it verifies**  
  That pages intended for indexing return 200 OK (or appropriate 2xx) and not 3xx/4xx/5xx, so they can be crawled and indexed normally.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“How HTTP status codes, and network and DNS errors affect Google Search”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/http-network-errors`

**Check 10.2 – Correct use of 404/410 for removed content**

- **What it verifies**  
  That truly removed or missing pages return 404 or 410 so Google can drop them from the index appropriately.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“How HTTP status codes, and network and DNS errors affect Google Search”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/http-network-errors`

**Check 10.3 – Temporary errors (5xx, timeouts) minimized**

- **What it verifies**  
  That 5xx errors and timeouts are rare, since frequent server errors can reduce crawl rate and cause temporary deindexing.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“How HTTP status codes, and network and DNS errors affect Google Search”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/http-network-errors`

**Check 10.4 – Consistency between mobile and desktop error handling**

- **What it verifies**  
  That error pages (4xx/5xx) return the same status for both mobile and desktop versions, avoiding mismatches for mobile-first indexing.[2]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Mobile-first indexing best practices”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/mobile/mobile-sites-mobile-first-indexing`

---

### 11. Blocked resources (CSS, JS, images, etc.)

**Check 11.1 – Key resources not blocked by robots.txt**

- **What it verifies**  
  That CSS, JavaScript, images, and other resources required to render primary content are not disallowed in robots.txt, so Google can render and understand the page properly.[1][2]  
- **Search Central topics**  
  - **“Robots.txt”**  
  - **“Mobile-first indexing best practices”** (“Let Google crawl your resources”)  
- **URLs**  
  - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots/intro`  
  - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/mobile/mobile-sites-mobile-first-indexing`

**Check 11.2 – JavaScript-based content is crawlable/renderable**

- **What it verifies**  
  That content loaded via JavaScript is accessible to Google’s web rendering service (no blocked JS, no required user interactions for primary content, etc.).[1][2]  
- **Search Central topics**  
  - **“JavaScript SEO basics”**  
  - **“Mobile-first indexing best practices”** (lazy-load & interaction)  
- **URLs**  
  - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/javascript/javascript-seo-basics`  
  - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/mobile/mobile-sites-mobile-first-indexing`

**Check 11.3 – Mobile and desktop versions expose same content/resources**

- **What it verifies**  
  That the mobile version does not hide or block important resources needed for indexing that are available on desktop, to avoid content mismatch under mobile-first indexing.[2]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Mobile-first indexing best practices”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/mobile/mobile-sites-mobile-first-indexing`

---

### 12. Misc core crawl/index controls (meta + links)

These are not separate from your list but are core crawlability checks Google emphasizes in the same docs.

**Check 12.1 – Links are crawlable (no JS-only navigation, proper `<a>` tags)**

- **What it verifies**  
  That internal links use HTML `<a>` elements with `href` attributes, are not blocked, and are not exclusively triggered by JS events; this allows Googlebot to discover pages.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Make your links crawlable”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/links-crawlable`

**Check 12.2 – Outbound link qualification (`rel` attributes)**

- **What it verifies**  
  That outbound links use `rel="nofollow"`, `sponsored`, or `ugc` as appropriate, without accidentally blocking important internal navigation through such attributes.[1]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Qualify your outbound links to Google with rel attributes”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/qualify-outbound-links`

**Check 12.3 – Page metadata is valid and parseable**

- **What it verifies**  
  That HTML metadata (title, meta description, meta robots) is valid, not duplicated incorrectly, and present on both mobile and desktop for mobile-first indexing.[1][2]  
- **Search Central topics**  
  - **“Use valid HTML to specify page metadata”**  
  - **“Mobile-first indexing best practices”**  
- **URLs**  
  - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/page-metadata`  
  - `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/mobile/mobile-sites-mobile-first-indexing`

---

### 13. High-level crawling/indexing fundamentals

You can also map tests back to Google’s high-level process to ensure app-level behavior aligns with expectations.

**Check 13.1 – Discovery, crawling, indexing flow**

- **What it verifies**  
  That pages can be discovered (internal links/sitemaps), crawled (not blocked, good responses), and then indexed (no `noindex`, reasonable content), matching Google’s three-stage model: crawling → indexing → serving.[3]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“Crawling and indexing”** (section overview)  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing`

**Check 13.2 – Understanding of how search works as context**

- **What it verifies**  
  That the site respects the overall model of how Google handles content, providing the background for all the granular checks above.[3]  
- **Search Central topic**  
  **“How Search works”**  
- **URL**  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/how-search-works`

---

If you want, I can next turn this into a tabular “audit spec” (one row per check with: ID, check description, how to test, pass/fail criteria, and mapping to these URLs) ready for QA/SEO tooling.
